issues.claim: Neither the original code nor the proposed Lua fix removes users from the set, so neither limits CONCURRENT users; both cap cumulative DISTINCT users per TTL window. The review misses this fundamental flaw.

evidence_pro.assertion: The original path adds an ID, checks SCARD, and calls chain.filter without any SREM or per-user reference-count decrement.
evidence_pro.impact: A completed request occupies a slot until the fixed TTL expires.

evidence_pro.assertion: The proposed script only reads membership/cardinality, adds IDs, and sets expiry; it has no release operation.
evidence_pro.impact: The replacement preserves the false 429 behavior for users that are no longer concurrent.

evidence_pro.assertion: The filter only ever runs SADD; there is no SREM on response completion, disconnect, or session end, so members accumulate until the whole set's TTL expires.
evidence_pro.impact: Once MAX_USERS distinct users appear within one TTL window the gate closes for everyone for the rest of the window regardless of how many are still active; the name 'ConcurrentUserLimiter' is wrong.

evidence_pro.assertion: The Lua script reproduces the same add-only model (SISMEMBER/SCARD/SADD/EXPIRE, no SREM), so it does not fix the core semantic flaw it implicitly promises to solve.
evidence_pro.impact: The 'fix' is a fixed-window distinct-user cap, not a concurrency limiter.

issues.claim: The proposed Lua script sets EXPIRE only when current_size==0, so a pre-existing non-empty set left without a TTL (e.g. leaked by the old buggy code) is never given one; it does not self-heal the leak the review claims to fix.
issues.location: analysis (recommended Lua: EXPIRE only when current_size==0)

evidence_pro.assertion: The original SADD and EXPIRE are separate operations, so a failure after SADD can leave a nonexpiring nonempty set.
The script calls EXPIRE only when current_size == 0, so it will not expire a surviving nonempty set with no TTL.
evidence_pro.impact: Deploying the replacement can leave an existing leaked set permanent, retaining IDs and potentially preserving a lockout.

evidence_pro.assertion: TTL is applied only at set creation; for any pre-existing non-empty set without a TTL, no code path ever calls EXPIRE again.
evidence_pro.precondition: A set already populated without an expiration (migration from the buggy version, or a historical partial failure).
evidence_pro.impact: Permanent memory leak persists; the script does not defensively re-apply TTL when missing.

evidence_pro.location: Lua script line: `if current_size == 0 then redis.call('EXPIRE', set_key, ttl_seconds) end`
evidence_pro.assertion: The script sets an expiration only if the set size was 0 prior to adding the user.
evidence_pro.precondition: When encountering a set that was leaked by the previous code (size > 0 but missing a TTL).
evidence_pro.impact: The set will never receive a TTL, and the memory leak/lockout will persist indefinitely. Changing the condition to `if redis.call('PTTL', set_key) == -1` would safely self-heal these orphaned sets.

issues.claim: In the original code TTL_KEY can outlive SET_KEY (SET_KEY expires before the equal-TTL TTL_KEY), so a later SADD that recreates SET_KEY finds the marker present and skips expire(), leaving the recreated set without expiration.

evidence_pro.assertion: The code expires SET_KEY before setting TTL_KEY with the same duration, so TTL_KEY's expiration is later.
If SET_KEY expires during that interval, a new SADD followed by hasKey(TTL_KEY) == true skips expire(SET_KEY).
evidence_pro.impact: If no later new user triggers initialization after TTL_KEY expires, the recreated set remains persistent.

issues.claim: The proposed Lua script returns 1 (allow) for any existing member before any size check, an undocumented behavior change versus the original (which rejected all requests when size>MAX) that the review does not flag.
issues.location: analysis (recommended Lua: SISMEMBER then return 1 before SCARD)

evidence_pro.assertion: The Lua returns 1 (allow) for any current member before any size check, so a returning user is never rejected.
evidence_pro.precondition: Set size already exceeds max_users (e.g. populated by a prior buggy deployment).
evidence_pro.impact: Original code rejected ALL requests (including returning users) when size>MAX; the Lua admits returning users. This is arguably more correct, but it is an undocumented behavior change the review presents as a faithful fix.

issues.claim: The review's 'reduce from five to one' round-trip claim overstates the typical cost: five operations occur only for the first-ever user; common paths use two or three.

evidence_pro.assertion: expire(SET_KEY) and set(TTL_KEY) run only inside the added==1 && !ttlMarkerExists branch; a returning user does SADD+SCARD (2 ops), a new user when the TTL marker already exists does SADD+hasKey+SCARD (3 ops).
evidence_pro.impact: Five round-trips is the worst case (first request only), not the steady-state cost the phrasing implies.

issues.claim: The review's #3 'Missing TTL (memory leak)' is overstated as permanent: the original code self-heals on the next NEW distinct user (hasKey(TTL_KEY) then false so expire re-runs); it persists only if all later traffic is from already-present users.

evidence_pro.assertion: If a crash leaves SET_KEY without a TTL, TTL_KEY was also never written, so the next distinct user (added==1) finds ttlMarkerExists=false and re-issues expire(SET_KEY,ttl).
evidence_pro.impact: The leak is repaired when any new distinct user arrives; it persists only if all subsequent traffic is from already-present users. The review presents it as an unconditional, lasting leak.
